report_name: "Miasma" Worm Spreads Through npm via "Phantom Gyp" Technique, Stealing Dev Secrets

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Supply Chain
attack_patterns.description: The Miasma worm compromised dozens of npm packages by embedding malicious commands in `binding.gyp` files, a technique dubbed 'Phantom Gyp', to infect downstream projects.

attack_patterns.name: JavaScript
attack_patterns.description: The Miasma worm executed malicious JavaScript code within the Node.js environment during the `npm install` process, triggered via a `binding.gyp` file.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials
attack_patterns.description: The Miasma worm was designed to search for and steal a wide range of unsecured credentials from developer environments, including secrets for GitHub, cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Azure), Kubernetes, and HashiCorp Vault.

attack_patterns.name: Exfiltration to Code Repository
attack_patterns.description: Stolen credentials and other sensitive data were exfiltrated by the Miasma worm to a GitHub account (`liuende501`) which served as a dead-drop location.

malware.name: Miasma
malware.description: A self-replicating worm and credential harvester that spreads through the npm registry using the 'Phantom Gyp' technique. It exfiltrates a wide range of developer credentials to a GitHub dead-drop location. It is considered a new iteration of the Mini Shai-Hulud malware family.

malware.name: Mini Shai-Hulud
malware.description: A malware family of which the Miasma worm is a new iteration.

tools.name: node-gyp
tools.description: A legitimate tool for compiling native Node.js addon modules. Its build process, which uses `binding.gyp` files, was abused by the Miasma worm to execute malicious code during package installation.

campaigns.name: Miasma Campaign
campaigns.description: A widespread software supply chain attack campaign active in June 2026, using the Miasma worm to compromise npm packages via the 'Phantom Gyp' technique for large-scale credential theft.

identities.name: Red Hat
identities.description: A victim of the Miasma supply chain attack, with 32 packages in its `@redhat-cloud-services` npm namespace being compromised.

identities.name: npm
identities.description: The public package registry targeted by the Miasma worm, where dozens of packages were compromised.

identities.name: GitHub
identities.description: Used as a dead-drop location for exfiltrated data. Also a target for credential theft (GitHub Actions secrets, npm tokens).

identities.name: AWS
identities.description: Cloud provider whose credentials were targeted for theft by the Miasma worm.

identities.name: GCP
identities.description: Cloud provider whose credentials were targeted for theft by the Miasma worm.

identities.name: Azure
identities.description: Cloud provider whose credentials were targeted for theft by the Miasma worm.

identities.name: HashiCorp
identities.description: Vendor of HashiCorp Vault, whose tokens were targeted for theft by the Miasma worm.

infrastructure.name: GitHub account liuende501
infrastructure.description: A GitHub account used as a dead-drop location for exfiltrating stolen credentials and other sensitive data.

indicators.name: Miasma Attack File Indicator 'binding.gyp'
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: Miasma Attack File Indicator 'binding.gyp'
id: a0b1c2d3-e4f5-4a6b-7c8d-9e0f1a2b3c4d
status: experimental
description: Detects the presence of a `binding.gyp` file. This file was abused in the Miasma 'Phantom Gyp' supply chain attack. Its presence in unexpected dependencies is highly suspicious.
references:
  - https://www.stepsecurity.io/blog/binding-gyp-npm-supply-chain-attack-spreads-like-worm
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-04
tags:
  - attack.t1195.002
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate npm packages that compile native addons.
level: low
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|endswith: '\binding.gyp'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the presence of a `binding.gyp` file, which was abused in the 'Phantom Gyp' supply chain attack. This file is legitimate for native Node.js addons but is suspicious in packages that should not have them.

indicators.name: Anomalous Child Process from npm Install
indicators.pattern:
title: Anomalous Child Process or Network Connection from npm
id: e8f9a0b1-c2d3-4e4f-b5c6-d7e8f9a0b1c2
status: experimental
description: Detects `npm install` processes that spawn unexpected child processes (e.g. curl, powershell) or initiate network connections, which may indicate a supply chain attack like Miasma.
references:
  - https://www.stepsecurity.io/blog/binding-gyp-npm-supply-chain-attack-spreads-like-worm
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-04
tags:
  - attack.t1195.002
  - attack.t1059.007
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate package installation scripts that perform network requests or run helper scripts.
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    ParentImage|endswith:
      - '\npm.cmd'
      - '\npm'
    Image|endswith:
      - '\curl.exe'
      - '\wget.exe'
      - '\powershell.exe'
      - '\pwsh.exe'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects `npm install` processes that spawn unexpected child processes or initiate network connections, which may indicate a supply chain attack like Miasma.

indicators.name: Unexpected Outbound Connection from CI/CD Runner to GitHub
indicators.pattern:
title: Unexpected Outbound Connection from CI/CD Runner to GitHub
id: f1a2b3c4-d5e6-4f7a-8b9c-0d1e2f3a4b5c
status: experimental
description: Detects outbound network connections to github.com from CI/CD runners during a build process. This could indicate data exfiltration to a GitHub dead-drop, as seen in the Miasma campaign.
references:
  - https://www.stepsecurity.io/blog/binding-gyp-npm-supply-chain-attack-spreads-like-worm
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-04
tags:
  - attack.t1567.001
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate build processes that fetch dependencies or source code from GitHub.
level: medium
logsource:
  category: network_connection
detection:
  selection:
    DestinationHostname|contains: 'github.com'
    # This rule requires context to identify CI/CD runner processes.
    # Example for a specific runner process name:
    # Image|endswith: '\runner.exe'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects outbound network connections to github.com from CI/CD runners during a build process. This could indicate data exfiltration to a GitHub dead-drop, as seen in the Miasma campaign.

relationships.source_name: Anomalous Child Process from npm Install
Unexpected Outbound Connection from CI/CD Runner to GitHub
